message.content:
Choosing between Python and Rust for a high-performance web backend is a classic engineering trade-off between **development velocity** and **runtime performance/resource efficiency**. Neither is universally "better"; the right choice depends on your specific constraints.

Here’s a detailed comparison across key dimensions:

---

## 🚀 Performance & Concurrency

| Aspect | Python | Rust |
|--------|--------|------|
| **Execution Model** | CPython uses a **Global Interpreter Lock (GIL)**, limiting true parallelism. Async frameworks (e.g., FastAPI, aiohttp) use **cooperative multitasking** (async/await) to handle I/O-bound concurrency efficiently. | No GIL. True multi-threading via OS threads. **Async runtime** (e.g., Tokio) provides highly efficient, single-threaded async I/O with fine-grained control. |
| **Throughput** | Good for I/O-bound workloads (APIs, DB queries). CPU-bound tasks are bottlenecked by GIL or require multiprocessing. | Excellent for both I/O-bound and **CPU-bound** workloads. Can saturate multiple cores without overhead. |
| **Latency** | Higher tail latency due to interpreter overhead, GC pauses, and GIL contention. | Predictable, low-latency execution. No garbage collector (deterministic memory management). |
| **Memory Usage** | Higher memory footprint per process. Objects are heavier. | Minimal memory overhead. Zero-cost abstractions. Fine-grained control over allocations. |

> ✅ **Rust wins** for CPU-intensive workloads, real-time systems, or when you need to maximize hardware utilization.  
> ✅ **Python can suffice** for I/O-bound APIs with moderate concurrency, especially if you use async frameworks and scale horizontally.

---

## ⚙️ Development Experience & Ecosystem

| Aspect |
